items.q: What is the best currywurst in Berlin for first-timers?
items.a: For most first-time visitors, Curry 61 is the easiest recommendation because it is central, highly rated, and close to Hackescher Markt. If you want the most famous names, compare Curry 36 at Mehringdamm with Konnopke's in Prenzlauer Berg.

Curry 36 is easier, open much later, and more convenient if you are in Kreuzberg. Konnopke's has the better historical setting under the U-Bahn tracks and a stronger East Berlin story. Choose Curry 36 for late-night convenience, Konnopke's for the classic Berlin food photo.

items.q: Where can I eat currywurst after the BerlinWalk tour?
items.a: The BerlinWalk tour ends near Hackescher Markt, and Curry 61 is the easiest nearby currywurst stop. It is a short walk from the end of the route and works well if you want a quick Berlin street-food lunch after the tour.

items.q: Is there vegan currywurst in Berlin?
items.a: Yes. Curry 61, Konnopke's, Curry 36, and Witty's all have vegan or vegetarian-friendly currywurst options depending on location and availability. Witty's is the strongest pick if organic and vegan options are the main priority.

items.q: What does mit Darm or ohne Darm mean?
items.a: `Mit Darm` means the sausage has casing, so it has more snap when you bite it. `Ohne Darm` means skinless, which is very common in Berlin currywurst. If you are curious, try both once and decide which texture you prefer.
Standard Berlin currywurst is usually mild. The curry powder is aromatic more than hot. If you want real heat, go to Curry & Chili in Wedding, where the spicy sauces are the main attraction.
